pattern: An element can only ever become a fixed point at a final index equal to its own value, and deleting elements only ever shifts later survivors to smaller indices -- so choosing which elements to keep as fixed points is really choosing a chain, in original order, whose values increase and whose 'index minus value' never decreases, which is a two-key longest-chain problem.
hints: An element at original position p can only ever land on final index v = nums[p] if v <= p (enough elements exist before it to shift left into place) -- elements that fail this can still be kept as filler, but can never themselves be a fixed point.

If you pick a sequence of original positions to be fixed points, their values must come out strictly increasing (final indices are assigned in order), and there must be enough real elements between consecutive picks to pad the gap -- that padding requirement is exactly p - v never decreasing along the chosen chain.

This is a longest-chain (like longest increasing subsequence) problem over pairs (value, position - value): dp[i] = 1 + the best dp[j] for any earlier eligible j with a strictly smaller value and a position-minus-value that is no larger; the answer is the best dp value (0 if nothing is eligible).

edge_inputs.description: statement example, every index becomes fixed
single element already a fixed point
single element that can never be a fixed point
already fully fixed, no deletions helpful
strictly decreasing values, nothing eligible except possibly the last
many duplicates of a value that can only fix one position
all zeros, only the first can ever be fixed

stress.description: random values against random positions, moderate size
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 2000))
    nums = [rng.randint(0, n + 5) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums]
